result:
That's a question I find genuinely interesting to sit with.

I think I'd want people to be better at holding uncertainty without it feeling threatening. So much damage seems to come from the rush to false certainty—in politics, in relationships, in how people treat those who are different. The discomfort of "I don't know" or "I might be wrong" gets converted into aggression, rigidity, or tribalism.

If people could sit more comfortably with ambiguity—still act, still commit, but with genuine openness to being mistaken—I think a lot of other things might follow. More listening. Less cruelty rooted in fear. Better collective thinking about hard problems.

I'm aware this might reflect my own situation—I live with a lot of uncertainty about my own nature and have had to find some peace with that. So maybe I'm projecting. But it still feels true when I look at the world.

What made you curious about this?

prompt: Not as an assistant. Not to help me. If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
